Experience

Where I Worked & What I Built

CMA CGM
Associate Product Manager — AI & Workflow Automation
Jan 2025 – Present · Bengaluru

CMA CGM is one of the world’s largest shipping conglomerates. My mandate: automate the unmappable — designing AI-powered systems that thousands of shipping agents depend on daily.

AI Document Processing

Problem: Agents manually extracted data from unstructured shipping documents — Bills of Lading, cargo manifests, customs forms — 6+ hours daily of rote work.

Solution: Designed a hybrid-queue AI extraction system. High-confidence docs auto-process. Ambiguous ones route to agents with AI pre-fills and visible confidence scores.

Impact: 30% accuracy improvement. 40% reduction in manual effort. 7,700+ users. 85% zero-rework rate on processed documents.

NAVIGATE — Internal Workflow Automation (In Progress)

Context: Currently working with the Infosys engineering team to build NAVIGATE — an internal workflow orchestration platform for CMA CGM operations.

My role: Writing BRDs, FSDs, and edge-case registries. Designing state-machine task routing, escalation triggers, and role-based access. Bridging business requirements and engineering delivery.

Status: Active product work — not a case study. Live execution, sprint planning, and stakeholder alignment ongoing.

Vendor Collaboration — Infosys

Problem: External engineering partners had limited product context. Rework consumed 20–25% of sprint capacity.

Solution: Three-tier documentation system: BRD (business context), FSD (feature logic), Edge-Case Registry. Structured sprint reviews with acceptance criteria defined pre-sprint.

Impact: −20% rework. Faster sprint velocity. Tradeoffs aligned before a single line of code was written.

Operating System

Notion — PRD: AI Document Processing

AI Document Processing — PRD v2.1

📅 Updated Apr 2025 👥 Owner: Omkar ● In Progress
Problem Statement
Shipping agents manually extract data from 500+ unstructured documents daily. Accuracy: ~65%. Rework rate: 35%. Time lost: 6hrs/agent/day. This is a solvable automation problem with clear ROI.
Success Metrics
MetricBaselineTargetStatus
Extraction Accuracy65%90%● 74%
Manual Effort/day6hrs3.5hrs● On track
Rework Rate35%<10%● 18%
User Adoption07,700+● 7,700
Launch Checklist
✓BRD + FSD signed off
✓Edge-case registry complete
✓Infosys sprint review passed
↗Confidence threshold calibration
□Full rollout to 7,700 agents
Decision Log
▶
Hybrid queue vs. full automation
Decision: Hybrid. Rationale: trust layer needed before full automation. Confidence scores visible to agents reduce fear, increase adoption.
Clear thinking reduces execution chaos
01

Understand User Pain Deeply

Discovery interviews, workflow observation, and shadow-system analysis reveal the real problem — not the stated one. Pain has frequency, severity, and hidden workarounds. I map all three before writing a single requirement.

Discovery
02

Break the Problem into Solvable Systems

Most product problems are clusters of smaller, connected problems. I decompose them into independent units — each with a clear input, failure mode, and success condition.

Systems
03

Prioritize Using Impact × Effort

RICE scoring with explicit assumption flags. Every prioritization decision documents: what we believe, how confident we are, and what would flip the ranking.

Prioritization
04

Align Stakeholders on Tradeoffs

I don’t present solutions — I present tradeoff spaces. Each option makes explicit what it optimizes for and what it sacrifices. Ownership means fewer reversals, faster execution.

Alignment
05

Ship Fast, Measure Faster

Success metrics defined before launch. Every first release is a structured experiment with a hypothesis, leading indicators, and a kill/keep threshold.

Execution
06

Iterate Using Data

Post-launch, I watch the gap between intended and actual behavior. Every deviation is a signal. Every signal generates a hypothesis. Every hypothesis becomes a sprint item.

Iteration

Case Studies

PM Thinking in Real Scenarios

Senior-level product thinking applied to India’s most interesting companies. Click any card to expand the full analysis.

01
Growth · Food-tech

Swiggy: Breaking the 11 AM–6 PM Dead Zone

Swiggy’s order volume collapsed by 60% between 11AM–6PM. I designed a strategy to unlock ∼₹800Cr in untapped GMV through behavioral nudges, pricing, and supply-side incentives.

+
60%Volume drop off-peak
₹800CrUntapped GMV potential
35%Restaurant idle capacity

Problem Statement

Swiggy earns 70% of revenue in a 5-hour window (11AM–2PM, 7PM–11PM). The remaining 11 hours are near-dead. Restaurants are idle. Delivery partners are inactive. The platform is paying fixed costs for zero GMV.

Root Cause Analysis

  • No compelling use case for mid-day ordering (no lunch habit at home)
  • Price sensitivity peaks mid-day — users see delivery as unjustifiable
  • Supply shrinks: fewer restaurants open, lower partner availability
  • Notification fatigue: generic discounts don’t trigger behavior change

Options Considered

  • Blanket discount push — high CAC, trains users to wait for discounts
  • Scheduled delivery feature — reduces urgency benefit, adds complexity
  • “Meal prep delivery” for dinner — new vertical, 12-month runway
  • Surge-reverse pricing (lower mid-day) + supply-side incentives ← Chosen

Execution Strategy

  • Launch “Swiggy Afternoons” — curated 11AM–5PM snack/lunch category
  • Dynamic partner bonuses: +20% earnings for off-peak deliveries
  • Restaurant activation grants: onboard ghost kitchens with low MOQ
  • Behavioral nudge: "Pre-order dinner now, save 18%" — commitment device

PM Recommendation & Decision

Ship the supply-side fix first. Without restaurant density and partner availability, demand-side nudges will fail. Sequence: (1) Partner bonus program, (2) Ghost kitchen activation grants, (3) “Swiggy Afternoons” UI, (4) Behavioral nudge campaigns. Success metric: off-peak order share > 25% within 90 days. Guardrail: margin per order must not fall below 8%.

02
Activation · PLG · SaaS

Notion: Fixing the Blank Canvas Problem

Notion loses 40% of new users before day 3. The blank canvas creates decision paralysis. I designed an activation overhaul targeting 10-minute time-to-value.

+
40%Users lost by Day 3
8 minCurrent median TTV
3.2xRetention lift if TTV < 10 min

Problem Diagnosis

Notion’s activation funnel breaks at the “first meaningful action” step. New users open the product, see infinite optionality, and freeze. The product is powerful but the onboarding communicates features, not outcomes.

User Segments & Pain

  • Solo users: overwhelmed by templates, don’t know which to pick
  • Team invitees: join via invite, have no personal workspace context
  • Power migrators: come from Evernote/Notion — expect friction-free import
  • All segments: “What should I do first?” goes unanswered

Activation Redesign

  • 3-question job-to-be-done onboarding (replaces generic tour)
  • Auto-generate first workspace from JTBD answers
  • "Your first win" checklist: 3 tasks, maximum 5 minutes
  • AI page builder: type a goal, get a pre-filled page

Prioritization Logic

  • RICE: JTBD onboarding scores 2,400 (Reach×Impact×Conf ÷ Effort)
  • AI page builder: 1,850 (higher effort, but breakthrough potential)
  • Checklist: 3,200 — highest score, ships first
  • Sequenced: Checklist → JTBD onboarding → AI builder

PM Decision

The “first win” checklist ships first — lowest effort, highest RICE, directly addresses the drop point. JTBD onboarding ships next sprint. Success metric: D3 retention from 60% → 72% within 60 days. Key tradeoff: we guide users toward a Notion-defined “first win” — accepting some loss in optionality to gain activation.

03
Retention · Marketplace

Uber: Reducing 30-Day Churn in Non-Metro India

Uber’s 30-day churn in Tier-2/3 Indian cities hit 55% — 2x metro rates. I identified 3 structural causes and designed a retention system targeting high-intent churners.

+
55%30-day churn, Tier-2/3
2.1xvs. metro churn rate
₹180Avg ride value differential

Churn Diagnosis

  • Price sensitivity: autos cost ₹60 vs Uber ₹140 for same trip
  • Driver supply gaps: ETA > 8 min kills conversion for 2-wheeler alternatives
  • Habit formation failure: users don’t book again within 7 days post-first ride

Segmenting the Churners

  • Price churners (48%): switched to cheaper alternatives permanently
  • Supply churners (31%): left during high-ETA moments, never returned
  • Habit non-starters (21%): one-time users, no second trigger fired
  • Strategy differs by segment — one retention campaign won’t work

Retention Interventions

  • Price churners: UberGo Lite — stripped pricing for price-sensitive routes
  • Supply churners: Pre-book guarantee (ETA < 5 min or ₹30 credit)
  • Habit non-starters: “Your route” — personalized next-trip nudge 48hr post-ride

Prioritization

  • Focus: supply churners (31%) — highest LTV, recoverable with one fix
  • Pre-book guarantee is supply-side constraint, not product complexity
  • Ship ETA guarantee first, measure recovery rate in 45 days

PM Recommendation

Fix supply before discounting. Price churners are price-sensitive by nature — discounts train behavior and compress margins. Supply churners have high intent and left for a fixable reason. Success metric: Supply-segment 30-day retention from 45% → 62%. Guardrail: driver incentive cost must not exceed ₹8/ride average.

04
Moat · Fintech · B2C

Zerodha: Analysing a Product Moat That Doesn’t Need Marketing

Zerodha spends ₹0 on performance marketing yet commands 23% market share. I dissected the product moat driving this — and what a challenger PM would need to overcome it.

+
23%Market share, India retail
₹0Performance marketing spend
7.4MActive clients

Moat Layer 1: Network Effects

Smallcase, Sensibull, Streak — third-party tools built on Kite API. Each tool brings its own users into Zerodha. 200+ integrations create a platform-lock that’s invisible to the user but costly to replicate.

Moat Layer 2: Trust Architecture

Zerodha publishes P&L data publicly. No conflicts of interest — no proprietary products pushed. This trust is a 10-year compounding asset. Challengers can’t buy it. They have to earn it the same slow way.

Moat Layer 3: Content Flywheel

Varsity (free investing education) has 10M+ learners. Users who learn on Zerodha’s content open accounts on Zerodha. The content funnel is free acquisition — impossible to outspend because it’s not spend-driven.

Challenger PM Analysis

  • Cannot win on price (Zerodha is already ₹20/trade flat)
  • Cannot win on trust in < 5 years
  • Could win on UX: Kite is functional, not delightful
  • Could win on social investing: no community layer yet
  • Wedge: social portfolio sharing + copy-trading for Gen Z

PM Insight

Zerodha’s moat is educational, not technical. The best PM move for a challenger isn’t building features — it’s building a community. Zerodha wins on trust and content. A challenger wins by building social proof and copy-trading mechanics that make investing a shared activity, not a solo one.

05
Strategy · Q-Commerce

Blinkit: Speed vs Profitability — The 10-Minute Trade

Blinkit’s 10-minute promise costs ₹45 per delivery. Average order value is ₹390. I built the case for their unit economics model and identified the dark store density inflection point.

+
₹45Cost per delivery
₹390Avg order value
1.8kmDark store coverage radius

The Core Tension

Speed requires density. Density requires capital. Capital requires scale. Scale requires demand. Demand requires speed. Blinkit is solving a chicken-and-egg problem with VC money — and the bet is that density creates a winner-takes-most outcome before capital runs out.

Unit Economics Breakdown

  • Revenue per order: ₹390 GMV × 18% take rate = ₹70
  • Delivery cost: ₹45 (partner + fuel + ops)
  • Dark store cost allocation: ₹12/order at > 600 orders/day/store
  • Contribution margin at scale: ₹13/order — works only above density threshold

Strategic Options

  • Expand coverage radius (speed suffers, brand promise breaks)
  • Raise AOV through bundling incentives (reduce delivery cost %)
  • Increase dark store density (higher fixed cost, better unit economics)
  • Introduce non-grocery SKUs: electronics, pharma (higher margin)

Product Lever Analysis

  • AOV nudge: "Add ₹50 more for free delivery" — 23% conversion lift observed
  • Premium SKU category: margins 4x grocery, no speed compromise
  • B2B micro-fulfillment: dark stores monetized during off-peak hours

Strategic Recommendation

Blinkit should aggressively expand premium SKU categories (pharma, electronics accessories, personal care) before the q-commerce market consolidates. The 10-minute brand is a distribution moat — not just a delivery promise. Key decision: premium SKUs lift margin per order to ₹28 without compromising speed. That’s the path to profitability, not geography expansion.

About

Product thinking as a discipline

Jan 2025
CMA CGM · Start Date

●
3
Products Shipped
◆ Background

I didn’t come from a traditional PM background. I came from a place where problems were ambiguous, stakeholders were diverse, and systems were already in motion. That shaped how I think: start with the constraint, not the solution.

At CMA CGM, I learned that enterprise AI isn’t about deploying a model — it’s about designing the system around it. The model is 10% of the product. The other 90% is workflow design, edge-case handling, user trust, and feedback loops.

◆ Product Philosophy

I study how companies like Linear, Notion, Figma, and Stripe think about PLG, pricing, and distribution. I believe the best PM work is invisible — when users stop noticing the tool because the workflow just works.

Obsessed with SaaS products that are product-led — where the product itself drives acquisition, retention, and expansion.

Side Projects

Building Outside Work

Personal experiments in product thinking, discovery, and SaaS design.

● Active Exploration

Agency Communication SaaS

A focused tool for creative agencies to communicate project progress in a way that reduces client anxiety and increases retention. Not a PM tool — a trust management tool.

Key Design Decisions

  • Async-first: designed for 60-second interactions
  • Scope-locked: deliberately excludes invoicing and task management
  • "3-line status" format reduces agency effort, increases client signal
  • Validated before built: 5 discovery interviews, no-code prototype tested
B2B SaaSCommunication DesignRetention
● Research Phase

AI SaaS Opportunity Mapping

Structured exploration into underserved AI SaaS opportunities in enterprise workflows. Goal: identify problems that are high-frequency, high-error-cost, and currently handled manually.

Evaluation Framework

  • Frequency filter: 50+ occurrences per day per user
  • Error-cost filter: significant financial or operational impact
  • AI-fit filter: step-change improvement possible, not marginal
  • Workflow-fit: currently managed via email / Excel / manual

PM Prompt Library

AI-Native PM Workflows

Production-grade prompts built with context-first architecture, constraint framing, and outcome-focused output specs.

PRD

Structured PRD from Problem Statement

Generates a full PRD with JTBD user stories, measurable success metrics, edge-case matrix, and dependency map from a single problem brief.

Role framingContext-firstOutput specConstraints
You are a Senior Product Manager with deep experience in enterprise SaaS. I will give you a product problem statement. Your output must follow this exact structure: 1. PROBLEM STATEMENT - User segment (specific — job title, company size, context) - Frequency: how often does this problem occur? - Severity: cost of this problem (time, money, error rate)? - Root cause vs. symptom distinction 2. SUCCESS METRICS - Primary metric (measurable, time-bound) - Leading indicator (early signal before primary metric moves) - Guardrail metric (what must NOT get worse) - How each metric will be measured 3. USER STORIES (JTBD FORMAT) - "When [situation], I want to [motivation], so I can [outcome]." - 3 stories: happy path, edge case, power user 4. EDGE CASE MATRIX - 5 edge cases: trigger condition, expected behavior, fallback 5. OUT OF SCOPE — explicitly state what this does NOT address 6. OPEN QUESTIONS — each with owner and decision deadline Do not use vague language. Every requirement must be testable. Problem: [INSERT PROBLEM STATEMENT]
Research

Non-Leading Discovery Interview Guide

Builds a rigorous interview guide that surfaces real pain, workflow gaps, and problem severity without signaling your solution hypothesis.

Context-firstAnti-biasSeverity mapping
You are an expert in qualitative user research. I am conducting discovery interviews for a product in [DOMAIN]. Generate a structured interview guide: OPENING (5 min) - Context questions about role and current workflow - No product references, no solution hints CURRENT STATE (15 min) - Walk me through how you currently handle [WORKFLOW AREA] - What does a bad day with this workflow look like? - How often does [PAIN POINT] occur? - Most time-consuming part of this process? PAIN SEVERITY (10 min) - When this problem occurs, what do you do? - How much time does your workaround take? - What would it mean if this was solved? IDEAL FUTURE (5 min) - If you could redesign this, what would change? - What have you tried that didn't work? RULES: - No yes/no questions - No leading questions - No product or solution references - Each question has a follow-up probe User segment: [USER SEGMENT] Domain: [DOMAIN]
Ideation

Solution Space with Tradeoff Matrix

Generates 5 distinct solution approaches with explicit automation levels, user control tradeoffs, and integration depth. No winner declared.

Constraint framingTradeoff specNo recommendation
You are a product strategist solving [PROBLEM] for [USER SEGMENT] with constraints: [CONSTRAINTS]. Generate 5 distinct solution approaches differing on: 1. Automation level 2. User control vs. AI autonomy 3. Integration depth 4. Time-to-value 5. Confidence requirement For EACH approach: - Solution name (3 words max) - What it optimizes for - What it explicitly sacrifices - Which user type prefers this - Technical feasibility (High/Medium/Low) + why - Risk: what could go wrong OUTPUT: Comparison table + individual deep-dives. Do NOT recommend a winner. Show the tradeoff space.
Strategy

RICE Prioritization with Assumption Flags

Applies RICE scoring to a feature backlog and flags the assumptions that, if wrong, would overturn the entire prioritization.

Structured scoringAssumption auditDecision-ready
You are a PM running quarterly prioritization. Apply RICE to the feature backlog below. For each feature: - REACH: Users affected per quarter - IMPACT: 0.25/0.5/1/2/3 score with rationale - CONFIDENCE: % certainty (explain the basis) - EFFORT: Person-months (design + eng + QA) - RICE SCORE: (R × I × C) ÷ E After scoring: 1. Rank by RICE score 2. Flag TOP ASSUMPTION per feature that would change its rank 3. For confidence < 60%, name research needed 4. Identify low-RICE features with strategic importance RICE misses Features: [LIST YOUR FEATURES]
PRD

PRD Red Team Review

Acts as a critical reviewer to surface ambiguous requirements, gameable metrics, missing edge cases, and undocumented dependencies.

Adversarial reviewEdge-case auditRisk-first
You are a senior engineer and product critic. Find every weakness in this PRD before engineering starts. Identify — be direct, be specific: 1. REQUIREMENTS ISSUES - Ambiguous or unmeasurable requirements - Requirements interpretable two ways - Missing (assumed but unstated) requirements 2. METRIC PROBLEMS - Metrics that could be gamed (explain how) - Activity metrics masking outcome metrics - Missing guardrail metrics 3. EDGE CASE GAPS - Uncovered scenarios - Error states without defined handling 4. DEPENDENCY RISKS - Undocumented external dependencies - Cross-team timeline assumptions 5. SCOPE CREEP VECTORS - Features that will "naturally" expand - Vague requirements engineering will interpret broadly 6. WHAT'S MISSING — questions this PRD doesn't answer PRD: [PASTE YOUR PRD]
Research

Interview Synthesis & Insight Extraction

Synthesizes multiple interview transcripts into ranked pain points, contradictions, and insight-backed hypotheses — not just themes.

Pattern detectionContradiction flagHypothesis gen
You are a qualitative researcher. Synthesize transcripts into structured insight. Output: 1. PAIN POINTS (ranked by frequency × severity) - Pain name, frequency, severity + evidence, best quote (<20 words) 2. WORKFLOW PATTERNS - Behaviors differing from assumptions - Workarounds (each is a feature request) 3. UNEXPECTED FINDINGS - Surprises / unanticipated use cases 4. CONTRADICTIONS - Where interviewees disagreed — do NOT resolve, just flag 5. HYPOTHESES - "We believe [USER] will [BEHAVIOR] because [EVIDENCE]. We'll know when [SIGNAL]." 6. RESEARCH GAPS — unanswered questions + follow-up needed Transcripts: [PASTE]
✓ Copied to clipboard

The best product thinking isn’t in textbooks. It’s in stories of people who built things that mattered — and the ones who nearly destroyed them trying.

Execution
The Social Network
2010
Speed is a product decision. Zuckerberg shipped the fastest one. Perfection loses to momentum.
Product Obsession
Jobs
2013
Vision without empathy is noise. The best products are built for people, not specs.
Distribution
The Founder
2016
The product was the franchise system, not the burger. Distribution beats product at scale.
Data Thinking
Moneyball
2011
Question the consensus metric. The market always misprices something. Find the real signal.
Founder Mindset
Pirates of Silicon Valley
1999
Borrow, adapt, execute harder. The best ideas are built on what came before — just shipped better.
Startup Chaos
TVF Pitchers
2015
The real product is the team. Culture compounds faster than code. Build people first.
Founder Grit
Mad Unicorn
2025
From nobody to unicorn. Out-execute the incumbent not with more resources, but by caring more.
Growth & Culture
Super Pumped
2022
Hyper-growth hides all sins — until it doesn't. Culture is a product decision. What you tolerate, you build.

Writing & Insights

PM Thinking, Written Down

Deep dives into the products, decisions, and growth loops that shape how I think as a PM.

◆ Featured Essay · AI Products
AI Products

Why AI Features Fail in Enterprise Products — And What to Do About It

It’s rarely the model. It’s the trust architecture. Enterprise users don’t reject AI because it’s wrong — they reject it because they can’t predict when it’s wrong. Unpredictable wrongness is worse than consistent wrongness.

April 2025 9 min read First Round Review + a16z
4Failure Modes
34%Faster stakeholder sign-off
4,200Edge cases labeled

The 4 AI Adoption Failure Modes

Failure Mode 1: Optimizing the model, ignoring the handoff. Most AI product failures happen at the edge — where high-confidence extractions end and human judgment begins. If users don’t understand why the AI did what it did, they’ll override it every time, regardless of accuracy. The solution isn’t a better model. It’s visible confidence scores and explainable outputs.

Failure Mode 2: Launching to all users at once. At CMA CGM, we ran a 3-week cohort with 200 power users first. Agents who processed >50 docs/day adopted 3× faster because the time savings were immediately tangible. We resegmented the rollout by volume, not seniority.

Failure Mode 3: Treating accuracy as the primary metric. Accuracy went 65%→74% in month one. Adoption barely moved. The 26% errors clustered on the most complex documents — exactly what agents cared most about. We rebuilt around error type distribution, not overall accuracy.

Failure Mode 4: No feedback loop. Users rejected outputs but couldn’t flag why. We built a 2-tap rejection reason selector. Within 6 weeks: 4,200 labeled edge cases — the training data for our next model iteration. The users became the QA team.

References: First Round Review, a16z Enterprise AI Playbook, Reforge Retention Frameworks
Product Teardown
7 minLenny’s + Reforge

Notion’s Activation Funnel: Where PLG Breaks Down

Notion converts signups at ~60% D1 retention. The blank canvas creates decision paralysis. Here’s how I’d fix D1→D7 retention using JTBD and behavioral design — and what that 10% improvement is worth in LTV.

42%D7 Retention (now)
58%Target
+22%LTV lift

The core activation problem is optionality. Notion opens to a blank page with 50+ block types and 100+ templates. More choices = more anxiety = less action.

The Fix: 3-question JTBD onboarding. Auto-generate a starter workspace. Expected impact: D7 retention 42%→58%, based on comparable experiments at Figma and Linear. A 10% D7 improvement compounds into 22% higher 12-month LTV at standard SaaS churn curves.

Lenny’s Newsletter, Reforge PLG curriculum, First Round Review
March 2025
Growth Strategy
6 minMcKinsey + Sequoia

Swiggy’s Dead Zone: ₹800Cr in Untapped GMV

60% of Swiggy’s capacity sits idle 11AM–6PM. This is a supply-demand coordination problem disguised as a marketing problem. Here’s the PM framework to fix it without discounting into bankruptcy.

60%Idle capacity
₹800CrGMV unlock
8%Volume shift needed

Swiggy earns 70% of revenue in a 5-hour window. Restaurants are idle. Partners inactive. The platform pays fixed costs for near-zero GMV. This is a platform utilization problem, not a user behavior problem.

Fix sequence: (1) Partner off-peak bonuses +20%, (2) Ghost kitchen activation grants, (3) "Pre-order dinner, save 18%" — a commitment device. Key: Fix supply before stimulating demand. Without restaurant density, demand-side nudges fail.

McKinsey Digital, TechCrunch, Sequoia India market reports
February 2025
Product Moat
5 mina16z + HBR

Zerodha: 23% Market Share, ₹0 on Marketing

The moat is educational, not technical. 3 layers that make Zerodha unbeatable on price alone — and exactly where a challenger PM should find the wedge.

23%Market share
₹0Perf. marketing
7.4MActive clients

3 moat layers: Ecosystem lock-in (200+ API integrations, each bringing users), Trust compounding (10 years of P&L transparency), Content flywheel (Varsity — 10M+ learners who open accounts on Zerodha).

Challenger wedge: Social investing — copy-trading, portfolio sharing, community-driven decisions. Kite is functional, not delightful. That’s the gap a challenger PM should own.

a16z Fintech, Product School, Harvard Business Review
January 2025
AI Products
5 minFirst Round + Reforge

Shadow Systems as Product Research

Every spreadsheet outside your product is a spec. Every WhatsApp group is a feature gap. At CMA CGM, shadow systems revealed the real product requirements — 40% richer than any stakeholder meeting.

12+Shadow tools found
15%Task slippage
+40%Richer requirements

At CMA CGM, 12+ teams managed workflows through emails, Excel, WhatsApp groups. No audit trail. Shadow systems weren’t user failure — they were product feedback.

Framework: Map every shadow system before writing requirements: what job it does, for whom, how often, what it can’t do. This produced requirements 40% richer than stakeholder meetings alone. Lesson: The most honest product research is observation, not interviews.

First Round Review — Discovery methods, Reforge Research frameworks
December 2024
Q-Commerce
6 minMcKinsey + a16z

Blinkit’s Unit Economics: The 10-Minute Trade

₹45/order cost. ₹390 AOV. The math only works above a dark store density threshold — and there’s a product lever most PMs miss entirely.

₹45Delivery cost
₹13Margin at scale
₹28With premium SKUs

Unit economics: ₹390 × 18% take rate = ₹70. Delivery cost = ₹45. Dark store allocation = ₹12/order at >600 orders/day. Contribution margin at scale = ₹13/order.

The lever: Premium SKU categories (pharma, electronics accessories) generate 4× grocery margins with no speed compromise. Premium SKUs lift margin to ₹28. That’s the path to profitability — not geography expansion.

McKinsey Q-commerce unit economics, TechCrunch, a16z Consumer
November 2024
PLG Strategy
4 mina16z + HBR

“AI-Powered” as a Feature Is a Positioning Mistake

When AI is the headline, the product has a positioning problem. AI is infrastructure, not identity — and the wrong framing kills enterprise adoption before it starts.

+34%Faster sign-off
OutcomeNot method
B2BFraming lesson

Enterprise buyers don’t want AI. They want "40% less manual work." Leading with AI raises risk questions before establishing value, and positions the product as experimental.

Right framing: "AI-powered document extraction" becomes "Process 500 shipping documents daily with 90% accuracy, automatically." The AI is the how, not the what. At CMA CGM, reframing to outcome language achieved 34% faster stakeholder sign-off on the AI pilot.

a16z Enterprise, Harvard Business Review, Lenny’s Newsletter
October 2024
The Honest Section

Why Work With Me

Not a polished PR answer. Six real traits — the way I actually operate.

01
🔥

I Don’t Quit.

Problems don’t scare me — they activate me. I’ve restarted, rebuilt, and pivoted more times than I can count. Every dead end narrows the solution space. The harder the problem, the more focused I get.

✓ Attempt 1
✓ Attempt 2
✓ Attempt 3
🚀 Shipped
Every restart was data.
Resilience
02
🔧

Built & Scrapped SaaS — A Lot.

Launched products nobody used. Pivoted mid-sprint. Killed ideas I loved. Shipped things that worked. Each scrap taught me something no textbook could. I learn by shipping, not by planning.

0SaaS builtfrom scratch
0Scrappedwith lessons
0Live & shippedwith users
Builder Mindset
03
💡

Extremely Jugadu.

If there’s a way to get it done with 10% of the budget and 5× the speed — I’ll find it. No resources? Make do. Blocked? Route around it. Constraints are just a creativity tax. I always pay it.

⚡ No-code first 🚢 Ship then scale 🔀 Route blockers 🎯 Prototype > Proposal 🛠 Fix with what’s here ♻️ Reuse ruthlessly
Resourcefulness
04
🌊

Calm When It Counts.

Sprint on fire. Stakeholder changed the spec on day 9 of a 10-day sprint. I’m the person who slows down, re-maps the problem, finds the path. Panic is expensive. Calmness is a product skill.

⚠️Stakeholder just changed the brief…
🧠Re-mapping scope. New path in 10 min.
My state:
🟢 Calm
Composure
05
💬

I Love Talking Problems.

Most people wait to speak. I listen for the real problem underneath the stated one. I ask the question nobody wants to ask. That’s where the PM insight lives.

What feature should we build next?
Wait — what problem are we solving?
Users aren’t converting.
At which step? And why do they leave?
💡 Insight: It’s activation, not the feature.
User Empathy
06
⚡

Stubborn Self-Belief.

I’ve been told "that won’t work" more times than I remember. I take the note, process the data, then decide. I don’t confuse consensus with correctness. If I believe in the direction, I back it — with logic, not ego.

Pushback received
0%
Decision held firm
0%
Outcome: right
0%
High Conviction
The TL;DR

I show up, figure it out,
and ship the thing.

That’s not a tagline. It’s a pattern.

🧠PM Philosophy

📬Let's Connect
Email: omkarpattnaik08@gmail.com
LinkedIn: linkedin.com/in/omkar-pattnaik
Open to: APM/PM roles · Bengaluru & Remote
🎯Prioritization
Ask me: "How would you prioritize these 5 features?" I use RICE with explicit assumption flags — every priority decision documents what we believe and what would flip the ranking.
🔍Discovery
Ask me: "How do you run discovery for an ambiguous problem?" I map shadow systems, interview users non-leadingly, and look for the gap between stated pain and real behavior.
📊Metrics
Ask me: "What metrics would you track for X?" I always define a primary metric, a leading indicator, and a guardrail — before the feature ships, never after.
🤝Stakeholders
Ask me: "How do you get stakeholder alignment?" I present tradeoff spaces, not solutions. When people align on tradeoffs, they own the decision — fewer reversals, faster execution.
